subject: Physics
chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Internal forces within a system can alter
options: the linear momentum but not the kinetic energy
neither the linear momentum nor the kinetic energy
both the linear momentum and the kinetic energy
the kinetic energy but not the linear momentum
questions.solution:
Internal forces in a system occur in equal and opposite pairs according to Newton's third law of motion. Therefore, the net internal force on the system is always zero.

Since the net internal force is zero, it cannot change the total linear momentum of the system.

However, internal forces can do work on the particles within the system. For example, during an explosion or when a compressed spring between two masses is released, internal forces convert potential energy into kinetic energy, thereby increasing the total kinetic energy of the system.

Thus, internal forces can alter the kinetic energy but not the linear momentum of the system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q:
Evaluate the following two statements concerning a system of particles:
(A) The linear momentum of the system is zero.
(B) The kinetic energy of the system is zero.
options: B implies A, but A does not imply B
A does not imply B, and B does not imply A
A implies B, but B does not imply A
A implies B, and B implies A
questions.solution:
The kinetic energy of a system of particles is given by $K = \sum \dfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2$.

If the kinetic energy of the system is zero, i.e., $K = 0$, then $v_i = 0$ for all particles since the kinetic energy of individual particles cannot be negative.

If all particles are at rest, the linear momentum of the system $\vec{P} = \sum m_i \vec{v}_i$ must also be zero. Thus, statement B implies statement A.

The linear momentum of a system is given by $\vec{P} = \sum m_i \vec{v}_i$.

If the linear momentum of the system is zero, it is possible that individual particles are moving such that their vector sum of momenta is zero (for example, two particles of equal mass moving with equal speeds in opposite directions). In this case, the kinetic energy of the system will be non-zero.

Thus, statement A does not imply statement B.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q:
Evaluate the following two statements:
(A) The linear momentum of the system is constant.
(B) The centre of mass of the system is at rest.
options: A implies B and B implies A
B implies A but A does not imply B
A implies B but B does not imply A
A does not imply B and B does not imply A
questions.solution:
The linear momentum of a system is given by $\vec{P} = M\vec{v}_{cm}$, where $M$ is the total mass and $\vec{v}_{cm}$ is the velocity of the centre of mass.

If the centre of mass is at rest (Statement B), then $\vec{v}_{cm} = 0$. This gives $\vec{P} = 0$, which is a constant. Thus, B implies A.

If the linear momentum of the system is constant (Statement A), then $\vec{v}_{cm}$ is a constant vector. This constant velocity can be non-zero, meaning the centre of mass may not be at rest. Thus, A does not imply B.

Therefore, B implies A but A does not imply B.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q:
Examine the following two equations:
(A) $\vec{R} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{i} m_i \vec{r}_i$
(B) $\vec{a}_{CM} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{M}$
In a noninertial reference frame,
options: both equations are correct
equation A is correct but B is incorrect
equation B is correct but A is incorrect
both equations are incorrect
questions.solution:
Equation (A) represents the definition of the position vector of the center of mass. It is a purely geometric relation and remains valid in all reference frames, whether inertial or non-inertial.

Equation (B) represents Newton's second law for a system of particles. In a non-inertial reference frame, pseudo forces must be taken into account along with the real external forces. The correct equation in a non-inertial frame is $M\vec{a}_{CM} = \vec{F}_{ext} + \vec{F}_{pseudo}$. Thus, equation (B) is incorrect in a non-inertial frame.

Therefore, equation A is correct but B is incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Imagine a system composed of two identical particles. One particle is stationary while the other possesses an acceleration $\vec{a}$. The acceleration of the centre of mass is
options: $2\vec{a}$
zero
$\dfrac{1}{2}\vec{a}$
$\vec{a}$
questions.solution:
Let the mass of each identical particle be $m$.

The acceleration of the first particle is $\vec{a}_{1} = 0$.

The acceleration of the second particle is $\vec{a}_{2} = \vec{a}$.

The acceleration of the centre of mass of the system is given by the formula:

$\vec{a}_{cm} = \dfrac{m_{1}\vec{a}_{1} + m_{2}\vec{a}_{2}}{m_{1} + m_{2}}$

Substituting the values, we get:

$\vec{a}_{cm} = \dfrac{m(0) + m(\vec{a})}{m + m}$

$\vec{a}_{cm} = \dfrac{m\vec{a}}{2m} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vec{a}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Suppose every particle of a body is positioned at a distance $R$ from the origin. The distance of the body's centre of mass from the origin is
options: $\geq R$
$\leq R$
questions.solution:
Let the origin be $O(0,0,0)$. The position vector of the $i$-th particle of mass $m_i$ is $\vec{r}_i$.

Given that every particle is at a distance $R$ from the origin, we have $|\vec{r}_i| = R$ for all $i$.

The position vector of the centre of mass is given by:

$\vec{r}_{cm} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$

Taking the magnitude on both sides, we get:

$|\vec{r}_{cm}| = \left| \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i} \right|$

Using the triangle inequality for vectors, $\left| \sum \vec{A}_i \right| \leq \sum |\vec{A}_i|$, we have:

$|\vec{r}_{cm}| \leq \dfrac{\sum m_i |\vec{r}_i|}{\sum m_i}$

Substituting $|\vec{r}_i| = R$:

$|\vec{r}_{cm}| \leq \dfrac{\sum m_i R}{\sum m_i}$

$|\vec{r}_{cm}| \leq R \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum m_i}$

$|\vec{r}_{cm}| \leq R$

Therefore, the distance of the centre of mass from the origin is $\leq R$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A body initially at rest fragments into two pieces of identical mass. The parts will move
options: along different lines
in opposite directions with equal speeds
in the same direction
in opposite directions with unequal speeds
questions.solution:
According to the law of conservation of linear momentum, the total initial momentum of the system is equal to the total final momentum.

Since the body is initially at rest, the initial momentum is zero.

Let the mass of the body be $2m$. It fragments into two pieces of identical mass $m$. Let their velocities be $\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$.

$\vec{P}_i = \vec{P}_f$

$0 = m\vec{v}_1 + m\vec{v}_2$

$\vec{v}_1 = -\vec{v}_2$

The negative sign indicates that the two pieces move in opposite directions. Since the magnitudes are equal, they move with equal speeds.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A uniform sphere is situated on a smooth horizontal surface, and a horizontal force $F$ is exerted on it at a distance $h$ above the surface. The acceleration of the centre
options: is maximum when $h = 2R$
is maximum when $h = 0$
is independent of $h$
is maximum when $h = R$
questions.solution:
For a sphere placed on a smooth horizontal surface, there is no frictional force acting between the sphere and the surface.

The only horizontal force acting on the sphere is the applied force $F$.

By Newton's second law of motion for the center of mass, the net external force is equal to the product of mass and the acceleration of the center of mass.

$F = Ma$

$a = \dfrac{F}{M}$

This expression shows that the linear acceleration $a$ of the center of the sphere depends only on the applied force $F$ and the mass $M$ of the sphere. It is completely independent of the height $h$ at which the force is applied.

The height $h$ determines the torque and thus the angular acceleration of the sphere, but it has no effect on the translational acceleration of the center of mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A ball placed inside a closed box travels around, making collisions with the walls. The box is situated on a smooth surface. The velocity of the centre of mass
options: of the ball relative to the box remains constant
of the box plus the ball system remains constant
of the box remains constant
of the ball remains constant
questions.solution:
Since the box is placed on a smooth surface, there is no external force acting on the system comprising the box and the ball in the horizontal direction.

According to the law of conservation of linear momentum, if the net external force on a system is zero, the velocity of its centre of mass remains constant.

The collisions between the ball and the walls of the box are internal forces, which do not affect the motion of the centre of mass of the system.

Therefore, the velocity of the centre of mass of the box plus the ball system remains constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: During a collision, the quantities that remain constant are
options: momentum and temperature, but not kinetic energy
momentum and kinetic energy, but not temperature
momentum, kinetic energy, and temperature
momentum, but neither kinetic energy nor temperature
questions.solution:
According to the law of conservation of momentum, the total momentum of an isolated system remains constant during any type of collision (elastic or inelastic), provided no external force acts on it.

Kinetic energy is not conserved in inelastic collisions, as a portion of it is converted into other forms of energy such as heat, sound, or internal energy. Even in perfectly elastic collisions, kinetic energy is not constant during the actual time of impact because some of it is temporarily converted into elastic potential energy of deformation.

The conversion of kinetic energy into heat energy in inelastic collisions leads to an increase in the internal energy, which changes the temperature of the system. Thus, temperature does not remain constant.

Therefore, only momentum remains constant during a collision, but neither kinetic energy nor temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A cannon fires a shell with a velocity $V$ at an angle $\theta$ relative to the horizontal direction. Upon reaching the highest point of its trajectory, the shell explodes into two pieces of equal mass. One of the pieces retraces its path back to the cannon. What is the speed of the other piece immediately following the explosion?
options: $V\cos\theta$
$\dfrac{3}{2}V\cos\theta$
$3V\cos\theta$
$2V\cos\theta$
questions.solution:
At the highest point of the trajectory, the vertical component of the velocity of the shell is zero. The velocity of the shell is purely horizontal and is given by $v = V \cos\theta$.

The momentum of the shell just before the explosion is $M V \cos\theta$, where $M$ is the mass of the shell.

After the explosion, the shell breaks into two equal pieces, each of mass $\dfrac{M}{2}$.

Since one of the pieces retraces its path back to the cannon, its velocity must be exactly reversed. Therefore, the velocity of this piece just after the explosion is $-V \cos\theta$.

Let the velocity of the second piece be $v_2$. By the principle of conservation of linear momentum in the horizontal direction:

$M V \cos\theta = \dfrac{M}{2}(-V \cos\theta) + \dfrac{M}{2}v_2$

Dividing the entire equation by $M$:

$V \cos\theta = -\dfrac{V \cos\theta}{2} + \dfrac{v_2}{2}$

Multiplying by $2$:

$2V \cos\theta = -V \cos\theta + v_2$

$v_2 = 3V \cos\theta$

Since the vertical momentum is also conserved and the first piece has zero vertical velocity, the second piece must also have zero vertical velocity. Thus, the speed of the other piece immediately following the explosion is $3V \cos\theta$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
options: the kinetic energy remains constant throughout
the initial kinetic energy equals the final kinetic energy
the final kinetic energy is less than the initial kinetic energy
the kinetic energy first increases and then decreases
questions.solution:
During an elastic collision, when the colliding bodies come into contact, they undergo temporary deformation.

During this deformation phase, a portion of the system's kinetic energy is converted into elastic potential energy. As a result, the kinetic energy of the system decreases temporarily.

As the bodies regain their original shapes, the stored elastic potential energy is completely converted back into kinetic energy.

Thus, the kinetic energy does not remain constant throughout the collision process; it first decreases and then increases back to its original value.

However, the total kinetic energy before the collision is exactly equal to the total kinetic energy after the collision.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
options: the kinetic energy initially increases and subsequently decreases
the kinetic energy remains constant
the initial kinetic energy matches the final kinetic energy
the final kinetic energy is lower than the initial kinetic energy
questions.solution:
In an inelastic collision, the kinetic energy of the system is not conserved. A portion of the initial kinetic energy is dissipated in the form of heat, sound, or deformation energy during the collision.

Therefore, the final kinetic energy of the system is always lower than the initial kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A circular plate of diameter $d$ is placed in contact with a square plate of edge $d$, as shown in the figure. The density of the material and the thickness are uniform throughout. The centre of mass of the composite system will be located
options: within the square plate
outside the system
at the point of contact
within the circular plate
questions.solution:
Let $\sigma$ be the mass per unit area of the plates.

Mass of the circular plate, $M_1 = \sigma \times \pi \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\sigma \pi d^2}{4}$

Mass of the square plate, $M_2 = \sigma \times d^2$

Let the point of contact of the two plates be the origin $(0,0)$.

The centre of mass of the circular plate is at $x_1 = -\dfrac{d}{2}$.

The centre of mass of the square plate is at $x_2 = \dfrac{d}{2}$.

The $x$-coordinate of the centre of mass of the composite system is given by:

$X_{cm} = \dfrac{M_1 x_1 + M_2 x_2}{M_1 + M_2}$

Substituting the values, we get:

$X_{cm} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\sigma \pi d^2}{4}\right) \left(-\dfrac{d}{2}\right) + (\sigma d^2) \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{\sigma \pi d^2}{4} + \sigma d^2}$

$X_{cm} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d}{2} \left(1 - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)}{1 + \dfrac{\pi}{4}} = \dfrac{d}{2} \left(\dfrac{4 - \pi}{4 + \pi}\right)$

Since $\pi \approx 3.14$, we have $4 - \pi > 0$, which implies $X_{cm} > 0$.

Because the point of contact is at $x = 0$ and the square plate lies in the region $x > 0$, the positive value of $X_{cm}$ indicates that the centre of mass of the system lies within the square plate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q:
Examine the two statements below:
Statement I: The linear momentum of a particle does not depend on the frame of reference.
Statement II: The kinetic energy of a particle does not depend on the frame of reference.
options: Statement I is incorrect, while Statement II is correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct, while Statement II is incorrect.
questions.solution:
The velocity of a particle depends on the frame of reference from which it is observed.

Linear momentum is given by $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$. Since velocity $\vec{v}$ is frame-dependent, linear momentum also depends on the frame of reference. Thus, Statement I is incorrect.

Kinetic energy is given by $K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$. Since speed $v$ is frame-dependent, kinetic energy also depends on the frame of reference. Thus, Statement II is incorrect.

Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A bullet strikes a block initially at rest on a smooth horizontal surface and becomes embedded within it. Which of the following quantities remains unchanged?
options: kinetic energy of the block
linear momentum of the block
temperature of the block
gravitational potential energy of the block
questions.solution:
The collision between the bullet and the block is perfectly inelastic.

As the block starts moving after the collision, its velocity increases from zero. Thus, both the kinetic energy and the linear momentum of the block change.

A portion of the initial kinetic energy of the bullet is dissipated as heat during the inelastic collision, which increases the temperature of the block.

Since the block moves on a horizontal surface, its vertical height remains constant. Therefore, the gravitational potential energy of the block remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A heavy ring of mass $m$ is secured on the periphery of a light circular disc. A small particle of equal mass is secured at the centre of the disc. The system is revolved such that the centre moves in a circle of radius $r$ with a uniform speed $v$. We can conclude that an external force
options: of $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ must act on the central particle
of $\dfrac{2mv^2}{r}$ must act on the system
of $\dfrac{2mv^2}{r}$ must act on the ring
of $\dfrac{2mv^2}{r}$ must act on the central particle
questions.solution:
The system consists of a heavy ring of mass $m$ and a small particle of mass $m$.

Since the ring is secured on the periphery of the circular disc, its centre of mass lies at the geometric centre of the disc. The small particle is also secured at the centre of the disc. Therefore, the centre of mass of the entire system coincides with the centre of the disc.

The total mass of the system is:
$M = m_{\text{ring}} + m_{\text{particle}} = m + m = 2m$

The centre of the disc (which is the centre of mass of the system) moves in a circle of radius $r$ with a uniform speed $v$. The centripetal acceleration of the centre of mass is:
$a_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$

According to Newton's second law for a system of particles, the net external force required to maintain this circular motion is given by the product of the total mass and the acceleration of the centre of mass:
$F_{\text{ext}} = M a_{\text{cm}} = 2m \left(\dfrac{v^2}{r}\right) = \dfrac{2mv^2}{r}$

This total external force must act on the system as a whole. Without further information about how the force is applied (e.g., via a string attached to the centre or the edge), we can only conclude that a net external force of $\dfrac{2mv^2}{r}$ must act on the system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A nucleus travelling with a velocity $\vec{v}$ emits an $\alpha$-particle. Let the velocities of the $\alpha$-particle and the residual nucleus be $\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$, and let their masses be $m_1$ and $m_2$, respectively. Which of the following statements is true?
options: $\vec{v}_1 + \vec{v}_2$ must be parallel to $\vec{v}$
No two of the vectors $\vec{v}$, $\vec{v}_1$, and $\vec{v}_2$ should be parallel to each other
$m_1 \vec{v}_1 + m_2 \vec{v}_2$ must be parallel to $\vec{v}$
$\vec{v}$, $\vec{v}_1$, and $\vec{v}_2$ must be parallel to one another
questions.solution:
According to the law of conservation of linear momentum, the total momentum of the system remains conserved since no external force acts on it.

Initial momentum of the nucleus = $(m_1 + m_2) \vec{v}$

Final momentum of the system after emission = $m_1 \vec{v}_1 + m_2 \vec{v}_2$

Equating the initial and final momentum:

$(m_1 + m_2) \vec{v} = m_1 \vec{v}_1 + m_2 \vec{v}_2$

Since $(m_1 + m_2)$ is a positive scalar quantity, the vector $m_1 \vec{v}_1 + m_2 \vec{v}_2$ is a scalar multiple of $\vec{v}$ and lies in the same direction.

Thus, $m_1 \vec{v}_1 + m_2 \vec{v}_2$ must be parallel to $\vec{v}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: While falling vertically downwards under gravity, a body breaks into two parts of unequal masses. The centre of mass of the two parts taken together shifts horizontally towards the
options: lighter piece
does not shift horizontally
depends on the vertical velocity at the time of breaking
heavier piece
questions.solution:
When a body falls vertically downwards under gravity, the only external force acting on the system is the gravitational force, which acts in the vertically downward direction.

There is no external force acting on the body in the horizontal direction. According to the principle of conservation of linear momentum, if the net external force acting on a system in a given direction is zero, the velocity of the centre of mass in that direction remains constant.

Since the initial horizontal velocity of the body is zero, the horizontal velocity of the centre of mass remains zero even after the body breaks into two parts. Therefore, the centre of mass of the two parts taken together does not shift horizontally.
